author: e-ddykim
title: JS API E2E Electron cleanup fails on Windows - Permission denied removing locked electron.exe
signature: rm cannot remove electron.exe Permission denied|Flaky Test

failed_jobs.name: JS API (24)
failed_jobs.symptom: E2E after-all hook fails: rm -rf cannot remove electron.exe with Permission denied

key_errors:
exec error: Error: Command failed: rm -rf demo-electron-app-project
rm: cannot remove 'demo-electron-app-project/node_modules/electron/dist/electron.exe': Permission denied

2 passing (35s)
1 failing
1) E2E testing for OpenVINO as an Electron dependency.
     "after all" hook for "should run electron package and verify output":
   Error: Command failed: rm -rf demo-electron-app-project
root_cause: The E2E tests (2 passing) completed successfully, but the 'after all' cleanup hook failed when trying to delete the demo-electron-app-project directory on Windows. The electron.exe binary in node_modules is still locked by the OS (Windows holds file locks on executables after they run), so rm -rf fails with EACCES/Permission Denied. This is a recurring Windows-specific flaky test infrastructure issue unrelated to the PR changes (GPU ConvertMatMulToFullyConnected fix). This is run attempt 2; a prior attempt already ran.
reproduction_steps: N/A - Windows file locking issue specific to CI runner environment
recommended_actions: Replace 'rm -rf' with a Windows-compatible cleanup (e.g., rimraf or a PowerShell Remove-Item -Force -Recurse with retry logic) in the E2E test afterAll hook
Add a brief delay or kill the electron process before attempting cleanup
Note: run_attempt is 2; do not re-run as this is already a retry
prevention_strategies: Fix the test cleanup to use a Windows-safe file removal approach that handles locked executables (e.g., use rimraf with retry, or PowerShell Remove-Item -Force with error handling). Track the JS binding E2E test ticket for a permanent fix.
historical_context: This exact failure (Permission denied on electron.exe cleanup) has been recorded 11 times previously in the CI Doctor MQ database under signature 85df26485ff568d8, first seen 2026-07-09. It is a well-known recurring Windows CI flaky test.
